title: Lightning Network
summary: Bitcoin's off-chain payment layer. The funding transaction and asymmetric commitment construction that gives a channel its security model, HTLCs and Sphinx onion routing for atomic multi-hop payments, the BOLT-7 gossip protocol that lets nodes find a route, watchtowers that punish breach attempts on offline channels, splicing and channel management, and the LSP layer that makes Lightning usable on a smartphone without taking custody.

edges.label: Payment channels and the funding transaction
edges.summary: How a 2-of-2 multisig funding output becomes a private ledger between two parties. Asymmetric commitment transactions, revocation secrets, the punishment mechanism, cooperative vs force close, and the to_self_delay window that the security model rests on.

edges.label: HTLCs, Sphinx onion routing, MPP
edges.summary: Hash Time-Locked Contracts as the primitive that lets a payment hop across multiple channels atomically without intermediaries being able to steal in flight. Sphinx onion encryption for sender anonymity. Multi-Path Payments and AMP for splitting large payments across routes.

edges.label: BOLT-7 gossip and the channel graph
edges.summary: How nodes propagate the public channel graph without a central directory. channel_announcement, channel_update, node_announcement, the on-chain anchoring that prevents fake channels, range queries for fast initial sync, and the privacy/efficiency trade-off that drives the use of private channels with route hints.

edges.label: Watchtowers and the offline-channel problem
edges.summary: Lightning's punishment mechanism only works if you are online to react. Watchtowers (BOLT-13 / The Eye of Satoshi) watch the chain on your behalf and broadcast a pre-signed justice transaction if a revoked commitment ever surfaces — without holding your keys, your channel balance, or knowing which channel triggered the response. Why mobile users need them and what eltoo would change.

edges.label: Splicing, fees, and operational realities
edges.summary: Channel sizing economics, splicing (BOLT-2 update_splice) for in-place capital adjustment without closing, fee policy (base_fee_msat and fee_rate_milli_msat) as the income side of routing, force-close cleanup of in-flight HTLCs, and what running a routing node actually requires day to day.

edges.label: LSPs, JIT channels, liquidity ads
edges.summary: Lightning Service Providers solve the inbound-liquidity bootstrap problem for new users by opening JIT channels on demand. The BLIP-51 / LSPS spec family standardizes channel-purchase and HTLC-interception APIs so a wallet can talk to any compliant LSP. Liquidity ads (BOLT-12 extension) turn liquidity into a market rather than a relationship — what makes Lightning usable on a smartphone without taking custody.
